# Military Events

Topics covering battles and campaigns of the American Revolutionary War and beyond in 1776.

<!-- BEGIN GENERATED: category-index — maintained by build-structure; do not edit by hand -->

| Topic | Description |
|---|---|
| [American Invasion of Canada (1775–1776)](american-invasion-of-canada-1776.md) | The Continental Army's September 1775–June 1776 campaign to seize Quebec Province; its failure at Quebec City and Trois-Rivières forced a full withdrawal but delayed Britain's northern offensive until 1777. |
| [Battle of Moore's Creek Bridge](battle-of-moores-creek-bridge-1776.md) | Fought February 27, 1776 near Wilmington, North Carolina; roughly 1,000 Patriot militia routed a Loyalist Highlander force in minutes, the first major southern Patriot victory and a spur to the Halifax Resolves. |
| [Continental Navy Establishment and Operations in 1776](continental-navy-1776.md) | The founding and first operations of the Continental Navy in 1776, including the March Raid of Nassau, the April Battle of Block Island, and John Paul Jones's cruises, which recovered military stores and established American sea power. |
| [Battle of Sullivan's Island](battle-of-sullivans-island.md) | Fought June 28, 1776 near Charleston, South Carolina; Colonel William Moultrie's palmetto-log fort repelled a British fleet, the first major Patriot victory and the first British defeat in the South. |
| [British Naval Operations in 1776](british-naval-operations-1776.md) | The Royal Navy's 1776 campaign of amphibious assault, river penetration, and coastal blockade, which established the naval supremacy underpinning British strategy from Sullivan's Island and Long Island onward. |
| [The Cherokee War of 1776](cherokee-war-1776.md) | The July 1776 Cherokee attacks on the southern frontier and the massive Patriot punitive expeditions that burned more than fifty towns, opening the Cherokee–American wars and Dragging Canoe's Chickamauga breakaway. |
| [Hessian Troops & Foreign Mercenaries (1776–1783)](hessian-troops-1776.md) | The roughly 30,000 German auxiliaries Britain hired from six territories beginning August 1776; they fought at Long Island, Fort Washington, and Trenton, and their hire was condemned in the Declaration of Independence. |
| [Battle of Long Island](battle-of-long-island.md) | The largest engagement of the American Revolutionary War by troop numbers, fought August 27, 1776 in Brooklyn, New York; ended in Washington's masterful nighttime evacuation across the East River. |
| [Battle of Kip's Bay](battle-of-kips-bay-1776.md) | Fought September 15, 1776 on Manhattan's East River shore; a British amphibious landing routed Connecticut militia and opened the way to the British occupation of New York City. |
| [Battle of Harlem Heights](battle-of-harlem-heights.md) | Fought September 16, 1776 in upper Manhattan; Washington's first tactical victory of the Revolutionary War, which restored Continental Army morale after the Kip's Bay rout and the loss of Long Island. |
| [The Great Fire of New York (1776)](great-fire-of-new-york-1776.md) | A fire on September 20–21, 1776 that destroyed up to a quarter of Manhattan days after the British occupation; its disputed cause fueled mutual arson accusations and shaped the seven-year occupation. |
| [Battle of Valcour Island](battle-of-valcour-island-1776.md) | Fought October 11, 1776 on Lake Champlain; Benedict Arnold's fleet was defeated but delayed General Carleton's advance until 1777 — a tactical British victory that became a strategic American one. |
| [Battle of White Plains](battle-of-white-plains.md) | Fought October 28, 1776 in Westchester County, New York; Howe drove Washington from Chatterton's Hill, but the Continental Army again withdrew intact, prolonging the war of maneuver north of Manhattan. |
| [Battle of Fort Washington](battle-of-fort-washington.md) | Fought November 16, 1776 on upper Manhattan; the storming of the island's last American fort cost nearly 2,900 prisoners and triggered the Continental Army's retreat across New Jersey. |
| [Battle of Trenton](battle-of-trenton.md) | Washington's surprise crossing of the Delaware River on December 26, 1776 resulted in the capture of a Hessian garrison, reversing Continental Army fortunes. |

<!-- END GENERATED: category-index -->
